question:
Problem A-3
Let \( p(x)=2+4 x+3 x^{2}+5 x^{3}+3 x^{4}+4 x^{5}+2 x^{6} \). For \( k \) with \( 0<k<5 \), define
\[
I_{k}=\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{k}}{p(x)} d x
\]

For which \( k \) is \( I_{k} \) smallest?
solution:
A-3.
Since the integral converges for \( -1<k<5 \), one can consider \( I_{k} \) to be defined on this open interval. Letting \( x=1 / t \), one finds that
\[
I_{k}=\int_{\infty}^{0} \frac{t^{-k}}{t^{-6} p(t)}\left(\frac{-d t}{t^{2}}\right)=\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{t^{4-k} d t}{p(t)}=I_{4-k}
\]

Then
\[
I_{k}=\left(I_{k}+I_{4-k}\right) / 2=\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\left[\left(x^{k}+x^{4-k}\right) / 2\right] d x}{p(x)} \geqslant \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{2} d x}{p(x)}=I_{2}
\]
since \( \left(x^{k}+x^{4-k}\right) / 2 \geqslant \sqrt{x^{k} \cdot x^{4-k}}=x^{2} \) by the Arithmetic Mean-Geometric Mean Inequality. Thus \( I_{k} \) is smallest for \( k=2 \).

descriptive_long.question:
Problem A-3
Let \( polynomf(variate)=2+4 variate+3 variate^{2}+5 variate^{3}+3 variate^{4}+4 variate^{5}+2 variate^{6} \). For \( exponent \) with \( 0<exponent<5 \), define
\[
integralexp=\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{variate^{exponent}}{polynomf(variate)} d variate
\]

For which \( exponent \) is \( integralexp \) smallest?
descriptive_long.solution:
A-3.
Since the integral converges for \( -1<exponent<5 \), one can consider \( integralexp \) to be defined on this open interval. Letting \( variate=1 / auxvar \), one finds that
\[
integralexp=\int_{\infty}^{0} \frac{auxvar^{-exponent}}{auxvar^{-6} polynomf(auxvar)}\left(\frac{-d auxvar}{auxvar^{2}}\right)=\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{auxvar^{4-exponent} d auxvar}{polynomf(auxvar)}=integralcmp
\]

Then
\[
integralexp=\left(integralexp+integralcmp\right) / 2=\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\left[\left(variate^{exponent}+variate^{4-exponent}\right) / 2\right] d variate}{polynomf(variate)} \geqslant \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{variate^{2} d variate}{polynomf(variate)}=integralref
\]
since \( \left(variate^{exponent}+variate^{4-exponent}\right) / 2 \geqslant \sqrt{variate^{exponent} \cdot variate^{4-exponent}}=variate^{2} \) by the Arithmetic Mean-Geometric Mean Inequality. Thus \( integralexp \) is smallest for \( exponent=2 \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem A-3
Let \( horseshoe(sandwich)=2+4 sandwich+3 sandwich^{2}+5 sandwich^{3}+3 sandwich^{4}+4 sandwich^{5}+2 sandwich^{6} \). For \( pineapple \) with \( 0<pineapple<5 \), define
\[
tangerine=\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{sandwich^{pineapple}}{horseshoe(sandwich)} d sandwich
\]

For which \( pineapple \) is \( tangerine \) smallest?
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-3.
Since the integral converges for \( -1<pineapple<5 \), one can consider \( tangerine \) to be defined on this open interval. Letting \( sandwich=1 / playhouse \), one finds that
\[
tangerine=\int_{\infty}^{0} \frac{playhouse^{-pineapple}}{playhouse^{-6} horseshoe(playhouse)}\left(\frac{-d playhouse}{playhouse^{2}}\right)=\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{playhouse^{4-pineapple} d playhouse}{horseshoe(playhouse)}=blueberry
\]
Then
\[
tangerine=\left(tangerine+blueberry\right) / 2=\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\left[\left(sandwich^{pineapple}+sandwich^{4-pineapple}\right) / 2\right] d sandwich}{horseshoe(sandwich)} \geqslant \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{sandwich^{2} d sandwich}{horseshoe(sandwich)}=raspberry
\]
since \( \left(sandwich^{pineapple}+sandwich^{4-pineapple}\right) / 2 \geqslant \sqrt{sandwich^{pineapple} \cdot sandwich^{4-pineapple}}=sandwich^{2} \) by the Arithmetic Mean-Geometric Mean Inequality. Thus \( tangerine \) is smallest for \( pineapple=2 \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem A-3
Let \( constantfn(constantval)=2+4 constantval+3 constantval^{2}+5 constantval^{3}+3 constantval^{4}+4 constantval^{5}+2 constantval^{6} \). For \( unchanged \) with \( 0<unchanged<5 \), define
\[
sumdiscrete=\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{constantval^{unchanged}}{constantfn(constantval)} d constantval
\]

For which \( unchanged \) is \( sumdiscrete \) smallest?
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-3.
Since the integral converges for \( -1<unchanged<5 \), one can consider \( sumdiscrete \) to be defined on this open interval. Letting \( constantval=1 / timeless \), one finds that
\[
sumdiscrete=\int_{\infty}^{0} \frac{timeless^{-unchanged}}{timeless^{-6} constantfn(timeless)}\left(\frac{-d timeless}{timeless^{2}}\right)=\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{timeless^{4-unchanged} d timeless}{constantfn(timeless)}=summirror
\]

Then
\[
sumdiscrete=\left(sumdiscrete+summirror\right) / 2=\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\left[\left(constantval^{unchanged}+constantval^{4-unchanged}\right) / 2\right] d constantval}{constantfn(constantval)} \geqslant \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{constantval^{2} d constantval}{constantfn(constantval)}=sumcenter
\]
since \( \left(constantval^{unchanged}+constantval^{4-unchanged}\right) / 2 \geqslant \sqrt{constantval^{unchanged} \cdot constantval^{4-unchanged}}=constantval^{2} \) by the Arithmetic Mean-Geometric Mean Inequality. Thus \( sumdiscrete \) is smallest for \( unchanged=2 \).

garbled_string.question:
Problem A-3
Let \( lkjhgfds(qzxwvtnp)=2+4 qzxwvtnp+3 qzxwvtnp^{2}+5 qzxwvtnp^{3}+3 qzxwvtnp^{4}+4 qzxwvtnp^{5}+2 qzxwvtnp^{6} \). For \( mnbvcxzq \) with \( 0<mnbvcxzq<5 \), define
\[
poiuytre=\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{qzxwvtnp^{mnbvcxzq}}{lkjhgfds(qzxwvtnp)} d qzxwvtnp
\]

For which \( mnbvcxzq \) is \( poiuytre \) smallest?
garbled_string.solution:
A-3.
Since the integral converges for \( -1<mnbvcxzq<5 \), one can consider \( poiuytre \) to be defined on this open interval. Letting \( qzxwvtnp=1 / hjgrksla \), one finds that
\[
poiuytre = \int_{\infty}^{0} \frac{hjgrksla^{-mnbvcxzq}}{hjgrksla^{-6} lkjhgfds(hjgrksla)}\left(\frac{-d hjgrksla}{hjgrksla^{2}}\right)=\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{hjgrksla^{4-mnbvcxzq} d hjgrksla}{lkjhgfds(hjgrksla)}=qazxswed
\]

Then
\[
poiuytre = \left(poiuytre + qazxswed\right) / 2 = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\left[\left(qzxwvtnp^{mnbvcxzq}+qzxwvtnp^{4-mnbvcxzq}\right) / 2\right] d qzxwvtnp}{lkjhgfds(qzxwvtnp)} \geqslant \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{qzxwvtnp^{2} d qzxwvtnp}{lkjhgfds(qzxwvtnp)} = plmoknij
\]
since \( \left(qzxwvtnp^{mnbvcxzq}+qzxwvtnp^{4-mnbvcxzq}\right) / 2 \geqslant \sqrt{qzxwvtnp^{mnbvcxzq} \cdot qzxwvtnp^{4-mnbvcxzq}}=qzxwvtnp^{2} \) by the Arithmetic Mean-Geometric Mean Inequality. Thus \( poiuytre \) is smallest for \( mnbvcxzq=2 \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
  p(x)=2+5x+4x^{2}+7x^{3}+4x^{4}+5x^{5}+2x^{6}.  

For every real parameter \alpha >0 and every real k with -1<k<6\alpha -1 define  
  I_{k}(\alpha )=\int _{0}^{\infty } \dfrac{x^{k}}{p(x)^{\alpha }}\;dx.  

(a)  Show that the integral converges precisely for  
  -1<k<6\alpha -1.  

(b)  Prove the reflection identity  
  I_{k}(\alpha )=I_{6\alpha -2-k}(\alpha ).  

(c)  Assume from now on \alpha >1/3 so that the open interval  
  (0,6\alpha -2) is non-empty.  
  Fix such an \alpha  and determine all k\in (0,6\alpha -2) at which I_{k}(\alpha ) attains its minimum value.  

(d)  Describe explicitly how the minimising k depends on \alpha , and list every \alpha >1/3 for which this minimiser is an integer.  

(e)  Analyse the complementary regime 0<\alpha \leq 1/3.  Prove that for these \alpha  the set  
  {I_{k}(\alpha ):k\in (0,6\alpha -2)}  
  is empty, hence has infimum +\infty , and consequently no minimum can be realised at either endpoint of the (degenerate) interval.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout denote d:=6 (the degree of p).  By inspection p has palindromic coefficients, whence  
  x^{d}\,p(1/x)=p(x)  for all x>0.  (0.1)

Step (a) - Domain of convergence.  
Near the origin p(x)=p(0)+O(x)=2+O(x), so  
  x^{k}/p(x)^{\alpha }=x^{k}/2^{\alpha }+O(x^{k+1}).  
Hence \int _{0}^{1} converges iff k>-1.

For large x one has p(x)=2x^{6}+O(x^{5}).  Therefore  
  p(x)^{\alpha }=2^{\alpha }x^{6\alpha }\bigl(1+O(x^{-1})\bigr),  
and  
  x^{k}/p(x)^{\alpha }=2^{-\alpha }x^{k-6\alpha }\bigl(1+o(1)\bigr).  
The tail integral \int _{1}^{\infty } converges iff k-6\alpha <-1, i.e. k<6\alpha -1.

Combining the two local conditions gives the announced strip  
  -1<k<6\alpha -1. \blacksquare 



Step (b) - Reflection identity.  
Substitute x=1/t.  Using (0.1) and dx=-t^{-2}dt,

I_{k}(\alpha )=\int _{\infty }^{0}\frac{t^{-k}}{t^{-6\alpha }p(t)^{\alpha }}\,(-t^{-2})dt
        =\int _{0}^{\infty }\frac{t^{6\alpha -2-k}}{p(t)^{\alpha }}\,dt
        =I_{6\alpha -2-k}(\alpha ). \blacksquare 



Step (c) - The minimiser for \alpha >1/3.

(i) Symmetry.  
Identity (b) shows that k\mapsto I_{k}(\alpha ) is symmetric about  
  k_{0}:=(6\alpha -2)/2=3\alpha -1.  (3.1)

(ii) Strict log-convexity.  
Let k_{1},k_{2}\in (-1,6\alpha -1), k_{1}\neq k_{2}, and \theta \in (0,1); set k_{\theta }=(1-\theta )k_{1}+\theta k_{2}.  
By Holder's inequality,

I_{k_{\theta }}(\alpha )=\int _{0}^{\infty }\!\frac{x^{k_{\theta }}}{p(x)^{\alpha }}dx
          \leq \Bigl(\int _{0}^{\infty }\!\frac{x^{k_{1}}}{p(x)^{\alpha }}dx\Bigr)^{1-\theta }
           \Bigl(\int _{0}^{\infty }\!\frac{x^{k_{2}}}{p(x)^{\alpha }}dx\Bigr)^{\theta }
          =I_{k_{1}}(\alpha )^{1-\theta }I_{k_{2}}(\alpha )^{\theta },

with strict inequality when k_{1}\neq k_{2}.  Hence ln I_{k}(\alpha ) is strictly convex, and the map k\mapsto I_{k}(\alpha )=exp(ln I_{k}(\alpha )) is strictly convex as well.

(iii) Uniqueness of the minimum.  
A strictly convex function symmetric about k_{0} has exactly one minimiser, namely k_{0}.  For \alpha >1/3 we have

-1<3\alpha -1<6\alpha -1 and 0<3\alpha -1<6\alpha -2,

so k_{0} lies inside both the convergence strip and the interval demanded in the question.  Consequently  

  k_{min}(\alpha )=3\alpha -1  for every \alpha >1/3. \blacksquare 



Step (d) - Dependence on \alpha  and integral minimisers.  
The minimiser is the linear function k_{min}(\alpha )=3\alpha -1.  It is integral iff 3\alpha -1\in \mathbb{Z}, i.e.

  \alpha =(m+1)/3 with m\in \mathbb{Z}.

Imposing \alpha >1/3 forces m\geq 1, whence the complete list

  \alpha =2/3,1,4/3,5/3,\ldots  (m=1,2,3,4,\ldots )  

with corresponding integer minima k_{min}=1,2,3,4,\ldots  \blacksquare 



Step (e) - The range 0<\alpha \leq 1/3.

For 0<\alpha <1/3 we have 6\alpha -2<0, so the open interval (0,6\alpha -2) is empty.  
For \alpha =1/3 one obtains 6\alpha -2=0, and the open interval (0,0) is again empty.

Therefore in either case the set of admissible indices

  S_{\alpha }:=(0,6\alpha -2)

is empty, and consequently

  inf {I_{k}(\alpha ):k\in S_{\alpha }} = inf \emptyset  = +\infty .

Because this infimum is infinite, it is certainly not realised by any k, let alone by an endpoint such as k=0.  In particular, although I_{0}(1/3)=\int _{0}^{\infty }p(x)^{-1/3}dx is finite, the value k=0 does not belong to S_{\alpha } and hence plays no role in the minimisation problem that was posed.

Summary for 0<\alpha \leq 1/3: the minimisation problem over (0,6\alpha -2) has no solution, and the infimum over that interval equals +\infty , so it is not attained at either endpoint. \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Extra parameter α introduces a two-variable family of integrals; the solver must work uniformly in α.
2. Denominator raised to an arbitrary power demands careful convergence analysis and produces the general reflection k↦6α−2−k, rather than the fixed reflection in the original problem.
3. Proving strict convexity of k↦I_{k}(α) by Hölder’s inequality is a deeper analytical step absent from the original A-3 (which relied only on AM–GM for two points).
4. Part (d) mixes discrete (integrality) and continuous (real α) aspects, adding a number-theoretic flavour.
5. Altogether the solution requires: asymptotic analysis, substitution symmetry, Hölder’s inequality, convexity theory, and a final diophantine argument—far more varied and sophisticated than in the original problem.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
  p(x)=2+5x+4x^{2}+7x^{3}+4x^{4}+5x^{5}+2x^{6}.  

For every real parameter \alpha >0 and every real k with -1<k<6\alpha -1 define  
  I_{k}(\alpha )=\int _{0}^{\infty } \dfrac{x^{k}}{p(x)^{\alpha }}\;dx.  

(a)  Show that the integral converges precisely for  
  -1<k<6\alpha -1.  

(b)  Prove the reflection identity  
  I_{k}(\alpha )=I_{6\alpha -2-k}(\alpha ).  

(c)  Assume from now on \alpha >1/3 so that the open interval  
  (0,6\alpha -2) is non-empty.  
  Fix such an \alpha  and determine all k\in (0,6\alpha -2) at which I_{k}(\alpha ) attains its minimum value.

  is empty, hence has infimum +\infty , and consequently no minimum can be realised at either endpoint of the (degenerate) interval.

        =\int _{0}^{\infty }\frac{t^{6\alpha -2-k}}{p(t)^{\alpha }}\,dt

(i) Symmetry.  
Identity (b) shows that k\mapsto I_{k}(\alpha ) is symmetric about

By Holder's inequality,

I_{k_{\theta }}(\alpha )=\int _{0}^{\infty }\!\frac{x^{k_{\theta }}}{p(x)^{\alpha }}dx
          \leq \Bigl(\int _{0}^{\infty }\!\frac{x^{k_{1}}}{p(x)^{\alpha }}dx\Bigr)^{1-\theta }
           \Bigl(\int _{0}^{\infty }\!\frac{x^{k_{2}}}{p(x)^{\alpha }}dx\Bigr)^{\theta }

(iii) Uniqueness of the minimum.  
A strictly convex function symmetric about k_{0} has exactly one minimiser, namely k_{0}.  For \alpha >1/3 we have

-1<3\alpha -1<6\alpha -1 and 0<3\alpha -1<6\alpha -2,

The minimiser is the linear function k_{min}(\alpha )=3\alpha -1.  It is integral iff 3\alpha -1\in \mathbb{Z}, i.e.

Imposing \alpha >1/3 forces m\geq 1, whence the complete list

For 0<\alpha <1/3 we have 6\alpha -2<0, so the open interval (0,6\alpha -2) is empty.

Therefore in either case the set of admissible indices

  S_{\alpha }:=(0,6\alpha -2)

  inf {I_{k}(\alpha ):k\in S_{\alpha }} = inf \emptyset  = +\infty .

Because this infimum is infinite, it is certainly not realised by any k, let alone by an endpoint such as k=0.  In particular, although I_{0}(1/3)=\int _{0}^{\infty }p(x)^{-1/3}dx is finite, the value k=0 does not belong to S_{\alpha } and hence plays no role in the minimisation problem that was posed.
